relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: After silence, Job opened his mouth and cursed the day of his birth.
His grief finally became speech.
He wished the day of his beginning could vanish.
He asked darkness to reclaim the day that first held him.
Deep shadow and cloud were called to cover it.
He wanted the night removed from the calendar of joy.
He asked that night to become barren and silent.
Even those who wake Leviathan were summoned to curse it.
He wished its dawn had never opened its eyes.
Because that night did not close the womb against his trouble.
Job’s why began at birth itself.
He questioned the tenderness that received and nursed him.
Death appeared to him as rest.
He imagined lying with kings and counselors.
The wealthy also rest there.
He wondered why he had not remained hidden before light.
In death, even the wicked cease their agitation.
Captives no longer hear the taskmaster.
Small and great meet there, and servants are free.
Job asked why light is given to those drowning in bitterness.
He described those who search for death like treasure.
The grave appears to them as relief.
He asked why light comes to one whose way is hidden and hedged in.
His sighing came before bread, and his groans poured like water.
What he dreaded had arrived.
Ease, quiet, and rest were gone; turmoil had come.
